1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
88	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
89	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
90	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
91	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
92	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
93	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
94	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
95	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
96	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
97	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
98	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
99	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
100	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
101	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
102	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
103	supplied.
104	
105	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
106	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
107	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
108	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
109	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
110	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
111	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
112	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
113	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
114	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
115	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
117	assembles the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units group is the
118	Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), exactly as Mathlib builds
119	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) as
120	\(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) over a fixed ring. The
121	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
122	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
123	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
124	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
125	
126	\section{Mathlib API survey}
127	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
128	
129	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
130	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
131	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
132	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
133	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
134	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
135	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
136	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
137	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
138	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
139	source.
140	
141	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
142	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
143	monoidal-category instance
144	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
145	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
146	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
147	\[
148	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
149	\]
150	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct` /
151	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
152	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
153	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).
154	% SOURCE QUOTE:
155	% "noncomputable instance monoidalCategoryStruct :
156	%     MonoidalCategoryStruct (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where
157	%   tensorObj := tensorObj
158	%   whiskerLeft _ _ _ g := tensorHom (𝟙 _) g
159	%   whiskerRight f _ := tensorHom f (𝟙 _)
160	%   ...
161	%   tensorUnit := unit _
162	%   associator M₁ M₂ M₃ := isoMk (fun _ ↦ α_ _ _ _) ...
163	% noncomputable instance monoidalCategory :
164	%     MonoidalCategory (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where ..."
165	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
166	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,
167	\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat}\) is exactly the project's
168	\(\mathtt{Sheaf.val}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) carrier (equal by \(\mathtt{rfl}\)),
169	so this monoidal structure applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
170	\(\mathcal{O}_X\) --- no project work. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
171	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
172	
173	\paragraph{(ii) The localization-of-monoidal-categories API.} Mathlib's
174	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Basic} provides the class
175	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) and the construction
176	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\). The class records exactly
177	the whiskering-stability data:
178	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal` and
179	% `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal`, in
180	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean` (L44, L86, L440)
181	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean).
182	% SOURCE QUOTE:
183	% "class IsMonoidal : Prop extends W.IsMultiplicative where
184	%   whiskerLeft (X : C) {Y₁ Y₂ : C} (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (hg : W g) : W (X ◁ g)
185	%   whiskerRight {X₁ X₂ : C} (f : X₁ ⟶ X₂) (hf : W f) (Y : C) : W (f ▷ Y)
186	%  ...
187	% def LocalizedMonoidal (L : C ⥤ D) (W : MorphismProperty C)
188	%     [W.IsMonoidal] [L.IsLocalization W] {unit : D} (_ : L.obj (𝟙_ C) ≅ unit) := D
189	%  ...
190	% noncomputable instance :
191	%     MonoidalCategory (LocalizedMonoidal L W ε) where ..."
192	Given \([\MonoidalCategory\,C]\), \([W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}]\), a localization
193	functor \(L : C \to D\) with \([L.\mathtt{IsLocalization}\,W]\) and an isomorphism
194	\(\varepsilon : L(\mathbf{1}_C) \cong \mathtt{unit}\), the localized category
195	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\,L\,W\,\varepsilon\) is a \(\MonoidalCategory\) with
196	\emph{all} coherence (associator, unitors, pentagon, triangle, naturalities)
197	derived. There is \emph{no} \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) anywhere in the
198	hypothesis chain.
199	
200	\paragraph{(iii) The proof template for $W$-monoidality.} Mathlib's
201	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
202	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
203	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
204	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
205	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:
206	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W`
207	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
208	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
209	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
210	% SOURCE QUOTE:
211	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
212	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
213	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
214	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
215	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
216	%     intro Φ
217	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
218	%     infer_instance)"
219	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
220	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
221	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
222	\(A\).
223	
224	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
225	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
226	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
227	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
228	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
229	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
230	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
231	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
232	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
233	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
234	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
235	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
236	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
237	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
238	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
239	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
240	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
241	critical path.
242	
243	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
244	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
245	is already present in Mathlib: the file
246	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
247	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
248	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
249	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
250	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
251	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
252	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
253	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
254	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
255	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
256	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
257	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
258	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
259	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
260	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
261	it is retained only to document the superseded route.
262	
263	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
264	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
265	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
266	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
267	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
268	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
269	independently of any upstream timing.
270	
271	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
272	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
273	
274	\begin{definition}
275	
276	\leanok
277	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
278	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
280	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
281	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
282	  \[
283	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
284	  \]
285	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
286	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
287	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
288	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
289	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
290	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
291	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
292	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
293	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
294	  canonical compatibility
295	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
296	     \;\cong\;
297	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
298	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
299	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
300	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
301	
302	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
303	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
304	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
305	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
306	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
307	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
308	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
309	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
310	\end{definition}
311	
312	\begin{lemma}
313	
314	\leanok
315	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
316	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
317	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
318	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
319	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
320	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
321	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
322	  \[
323	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
324	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
325	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
326	  \]
327	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
328	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
329	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
330	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
331	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
332	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
333	  \[
334	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
335	    \qquad
336	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
337	  \]
338	  the associator
339	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
340	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
341	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
342	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
343	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{proof}
347	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
348	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
349	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
350	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
351	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
352	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
353	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
354	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
355	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
356	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
357	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
358	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
359	\end{proof}
360	
361	\begin{remark}
362	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
363	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
364	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
365	        lem:whisker_of_W}
366	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
367	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
368	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
369	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
370	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
371	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
372	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
373	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
374	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
375	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
376	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
377	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
378	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
379	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
380	
381	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
382	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
383	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
384	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
385	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
386	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
387	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
388	  fixed-base presheaves in
389	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
390	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
391	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
392	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
393	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
394	  it.
395	
396	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
397	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
398	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
399	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
400	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
401	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
402	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
403	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
404	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
405	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
406	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
407	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
408	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
409	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). This mirrors Mathlib's own
410	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
411	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
412	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\): there
413	  over a fixed ring, here over iso-classes of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\).
414	\end{remark}
415	
416	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
417	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
418	
419	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
420	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
421	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
422	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
423	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
424	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
425	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes ---
426	under route~(e) these are read off the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure of
427	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} --- together with the closure of \(\otimes\) on the
428	locally-trivial \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype
429	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and the interaction with the pullback
430	functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
431	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the critical path of the PRIMARY
432	locally-trivial group law (it is consumed by the PRIMARY proof of
433	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}); it is dispensable only under the
434	route~(e) stalkwise fallback.
435	
436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	\leanok
439	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
440	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
441	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
442	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
443	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
444	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
445	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
446	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
447	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
448	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
449	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
450	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
451	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
452	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
453	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
454	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
455	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
456	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
457	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
458	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
459	  \[
460	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
461	      \;\;:=\;\;
462	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
463	  \]
464	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
465	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
466	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
467	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
468	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
469	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
470	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
471	\end{lemma}
472	
473	\begin{proof}
474	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
475	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
476	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
477	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
478	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
479	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
480	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
481	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
482	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
483	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
484	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
485	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
486	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
487	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
488	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
489	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
490	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
491	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
492	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
493	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
494	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
495	
496	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
497	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
498	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
499	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
500	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
501	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
502	\end{proof}
503	
504	\begin{lemma}
505	
506	\leanok
507	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
508	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
510	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
511	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
512	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
513	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
514	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
515	  morphism
516	  \[
517	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
518	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
519	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
520	  \]
521	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
522	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
523	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
524	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
525	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
526	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
527	\end{lemma}
528	
529	\begin{proof}
530	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
531	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
532	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
533	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
534	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
535	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
536	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
537	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
538	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
539	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
540	  isomorphism}
541	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
542	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
543	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
544	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
545	  therefore extension of scalars along
546	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
547	
548	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
549	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
550	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
551	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
552	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
553	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
554	  the abstract pullback functor.
555	
556	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
557	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
558	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
559	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
560	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
561	   \;\cong\;
562	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
563	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
564	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
565	  comparison
566	  \[
567	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
568	      \;\longrightarrow\;
569	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
570	      \;\otimes\;
571	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
572	  \]
573	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
574	
575	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
576	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
577	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
578	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned
579	  commit. The closure passes through two presheaf-level ingredients, of which
580	  exactly one now remains open.
581	
582	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
583	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
584	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
585	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
586	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
587	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
588	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
589	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
590	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
591	  \otimes_S N)\). This was the former ``H2'' bottom gap.
592	
593	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
594	  \[
595	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
596	  \]
597	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (``H1''). One exhibits a
598	  presheaf-level adjunction
599	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
600	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
601	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
602	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
603	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
604	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
605	  composing the resulting comparison with
606	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
607	
608	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
609	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
610	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
611	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
612	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
613	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
614	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
615	  presheaf level. The depth of this sub-chain is \emph{uncertain} and may span
616	  several iterations.
617	
618	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law: the
619	  direct gluing construction of the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
620	  invokes it (twice per cover member) to descend to the canonical presheaf-level
621	  associator. Closing the H1 residual is therefore the sole remaining substrate
622	  obligation.
623	\end{proof}
624	
625	\begin{lemma}
626	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
627	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
628	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
629	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
630	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
631	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
632	  product: the canonical map
633	  \[
634	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
635	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
636	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
637	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
638	  \]
639	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
640	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
641	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
642	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
643	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
644	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
645	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
646	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient (formerly
647	  ``H2'') of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
648	  adjunction (``H1'') as the sole open residual there.
649	\end{lemma}

651	\begin{lemma}
652	
653	\leanok
654	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
655	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
656	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
657	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
658	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
659	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
660	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
661	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
662	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
663	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
664	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
665	\end{lemma}
666	
667	\begin{proof}
668	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
669	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
670	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
671	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
672	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
673	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
674	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
675	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
676	\end{proof}

689	\begin{lemma}
690	
691	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
692	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
693	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_flat}
694	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
695	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
696	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
697	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
698	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
699	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
700	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
701	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
702	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
703	  left-whiskered morphism
704	  \[
705	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
706	  \]
707	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
708	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
709	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
710	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
711	
712	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
713	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
714	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
715	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
716	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
717	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
718	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
719	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
720	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
721	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
722	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
723	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
724	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
725	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
726	\end{lemma}
727	
728	\begin{proof}
729	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
730	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
731	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
732	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
733	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
734	
735	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
736	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
737	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
738	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
739	  \[
740	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
741	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
742	  \]
743	
744	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
745	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
746	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
747	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
748	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
749	
750	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
751	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
752	  linear maps (Mathlib
753	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
754	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
755	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
756	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
757	
758	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
759	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
760	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
761	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
762	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
763	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
764	\end{proof}

1801	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
1802	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
1803	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
1804	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
1805	  \[
1806	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
1807	  \]
1808	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
1809	  abelian-group structure with addition

1811	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
1812	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
1813	  inverse
1814	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
1815	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
1816	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1817	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
1818	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
1819	  map
1820	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
1821	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
1822	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1823	\end{theorem}

1834	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
1835	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
1836	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
1837	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
1838	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
1839	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on

1131	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
1132	the route~(e) target: a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on all of
1133	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\). It is not
1134	needed. The group law consumes only the existence of the associator, unitor and
1135	braiding isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, à la Mathlib's
1136	\(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\)), and the associator is now built directly by
1137	gluing (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). This
1138	block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be formalized;
1139	no Lean declaration realizes it and none should.

1141	\begin{lemma}
1142	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1143	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1144	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1145	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1146	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1147	  instance
1148	  \[
1149	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1150	  \]
1151	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1152	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1153	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1154	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1155	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1156	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1157	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1158	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1159	  category
1160	  \[
1161	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1162	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1163	  \]
1164	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1165	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1166	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1167	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1168	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1169	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1170	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1171	  composites.
1172	
1173	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it is currently
1174	  \emph{stated-but-unformalized} (no Lean declaration realizes the
1175	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance yet), pending the sole open obligation
1176	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1177	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1178	\end{lemma}
1179	
1180	\begin{proof}
1181	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1182	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1183	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1184	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1185	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1186	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1187	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1188	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1189	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1190	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1191	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1192	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1193	  at any point.
1194	\end{proof}

1196	\begin{lemma}

1199	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
1200	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1201	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1202	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1203	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1204	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1205	  \[
1206	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1207	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1208	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1209	  \]
1210	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1211	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1212	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1213	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1214	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1215	  ever required.
1216	
1217	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1218	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1219	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for
1220	  arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; the hypotheses are retained only to match
1221	  the carrier the group law multiplies on, and are not used in the gluing.
1222	\end{lemma}
1223	
1224	\begin{proof}
1225	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1226	  The isomorphism is assembled by \emph{gluing canonical local isomorphisms}. The
1227	  construction rests on \emph{no} whiskering of the sheafification localizer,
1228	  \emph{no} instance \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and \emph{no} stalk
1229	  computation.

903	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
904	  linearity half of ingredient (d.1) of
905	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- consumed by the
906	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proof of that lemma. They build on the
907	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
908	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.

911	\begin{proof}
912	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
913	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
914	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
915	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
916	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
917	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
918	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
919	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
920	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
921	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
922	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
923	\end{proof}

926	the load-bearing whiskering-stability field of the abandoned
927	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route to the
928	associator (route~(e)). The group law on iso-classes consumes only the
929	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), which
930	is now built directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
931	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
932	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) with no whiskering and no stalks. This lemma ---
933	including both its PRIMARY and FALLBACK proof routes and the stalk ingredients
934	(d.1)/(d.2) below --- is therefore off the critical path; its Lean declaration is
935	being removed, and no future prover should re-attempt it. Retained for the
936	historical record only.
937	
938	\begin{lemma}
939	
940	\leanok
941	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
942	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
943	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W}
944	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
945	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
946	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
947	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
948	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
949	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
950	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
951	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
952	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
953	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
954	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
955	  left-whiskered morphism
956	  \[
957	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
958	  \]
959	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
960	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
961	
962	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
963	  instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the load-bearing
964	  field of the \emph{full-generality} route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path
965	  (arbitrary \(F\)). For the relative Picard group law, however, the whisker object
966	  \(F\) supplied by the sole consumer --- the locally-trivial-scoped associator
967	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- is always \emph{locally trivial}
968	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), and in that case local injectivity
969	  of \(F \triangleleft g\) admits a markedly simpler, stalk-free proof, given as the
970	  PRIMARY route below. The general stalkwise argument (ingredients (d.1)/(d.2)) is
971	  retained only as the FALLBACK, required if the full route~(e) monoidal category on
972	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is later wanted; the locally-trivial
973	  primary route already suffices for the group law.
974	\end{lemma}
975	
976	\begin{proof}
977	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
978	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
979	
980	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
981	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
982	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
983	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
984	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
985	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
986	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
987	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
988	  On such a \(V\):
989	  \begin{itemize}
990	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
991	      gives a canonical isomorphism
992	      \[
993	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
994	      \]
995	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
996	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
997	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
998	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
999	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1000	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1001	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1002	  \end{itemize}
1003	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1004	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1005	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1006	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1007	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1008	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1009	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1010	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1011	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1012	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1013	
1014	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1015	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1016	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1017	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1018	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1019	
1020	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1021	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1022	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1023	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1024	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1025	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1026	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1027	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1028	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1029	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1030	  \[
1031	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1032	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1033	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1034	  \]
1035	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1036	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1037	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1038	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1039	
1040	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1041	  pinned commit:
1042	  \begin{enumerate}
1043	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1044	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1045	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1046	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1047	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1048	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1049	      above.
1050	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1051	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1052	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1053	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1054	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1055	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1056	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1057	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1058	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1059	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1060	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1061	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1062	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1063	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1064	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1065	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1066	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1067	  \end{enumerate}
1068	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1069	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1070	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1071	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1072	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1073	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1074	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1075	
1076	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1077	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1078	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1079	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1080	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1081	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1082	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1083	\end{proof}
1084	
1085	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma
1086	supplies the two whiskering-stability fields of the abandoned
1087	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}). The group law
1088	needs no such instance: the associator is built directly by gluing canonical local
1089	isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
1090	This block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be
1091	formalized; its Lean declarations are being removed.
1092	
1093	\begin{lemma}
1094	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1095	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1096	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_W, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_W}
1097	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1098	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1099	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1100	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1101	  \(J.W\):
1102	  \[
1103	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1104	    \qquad
1105	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1106	  \]
1107	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1108	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1109	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1110	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1111	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1112	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1113	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1114	\end{lemma}
1115	
1116	\begin{proof}
1117	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1118	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1119	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1120	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1121	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1122	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1123	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1124	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1125	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1126	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1127	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1128	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1129	\end{proof}

17	/-!
18	# The Picard group of a commutative ring
19	
20	This file defines the Picard group `CommRing.Pic R` of a commutative ring `R` as the type of
21	invertible `R`-modules (in the sense that `M` is invertible if there exists another `R`-module
22	`N` such that `M ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] R`) up to isomorphism, equipped with tensor product as multiplication.
23	
24	## Main definition
25	
26	- `Module.Invertible R M` says that the canonical map `Mᵛ ⊗[R] M → R` is an isomorphism.
27	  To show that `M` is invertible, it suffices to provide an arbitrary `R`-module `N`
28	  and an isomorphism `N ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[R] R`, see `Module.Invertible.right`.
29	
30	- `ClassGroup.equivPic`: the class group of a domain is isomorphic to the Picard group.
31	
32	## Main results
33	
34	- An invertible module is finite and projective (provided as instances).
35	
36	- `Module.Invertible.free_iff_linearEquiv`: an invertible module is free iff it is isomorphic to
37	  the ring, i.e. its class is trivial in the Picard group.
38	
39	- `Submodule.ker_unitsToPic`, `Submodule.range_unitsToPic`: exactness of the sequence
40	  `1 → Rˣ → Aˣ → (Submodule R A)ˣ → Pic R → Pic A` at the last two spots.
41	  See Theorem 2.4 in [RobertsSingh1993] or Exercise I.3.7(iv) and Proposition I.3.5 in [Weibel2013].
42	
43	## References
44	
45	- https://qchu.wordpress.com/2014/10/19/the-picard-groups/
46	- https://mathoverflow.net/questions/13768/what-is-the-right-definition-of-the-picard-group-of-a-commutative-ring
47	- https://mathoverflow.net/questions/375725/picard-group-vs-class-group
48	- [Weibel2013], https://sites.math.rutgers.edu/~weibel/Kbook/Kbook.I.pdf
49	- [Stacks: Picard groups of rings](https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0AFW)
50	
51	## TODO
52	
53	Show:
54	- Invertible modules over a commutative ring have the same cardinality as the ring.
55	
56	- Establish other characterizations of invertible modules, e.g. they are modules that
57	  become free of rank one when localized at every prime ideal.
58	  See [Stacks: Finite projective modules](https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/00NX).
59	- Connect to invertible sheaves on `Spec R`. More generally, connect projective `R`-modules of
60	  constant finite rank to locally free sheaves on `Spec R`.
61	- Exhibit isomorphism with sheaf cohomology `H¹(Spec R, 𝓞ˣ)`.
62	-/
63	
64	@[expose] public section
65	
66	open TensorProduct
67	
68	universe u v
69	
70	variable (R : Type u) (M : Type v) (N P Q A : Type*) [CommSemiring R]
71	variable [AddCommMonoid M] [AddCommMonoid N] [AddCommMonoid P] [AddCommMonoid Q]
72	variable [Module R M] [Module R N] [Module R P] [Module R Q]
73	
74	namespace Module
75	
76	/-- An `R`-module `M` is invertible if the canonical map `Mᵛ ⊗[R] M → R` is an isomorphism,
77	where `Mᵛ` is the `R`-dual of `M`. -/
78	protected class Invertible : Prop where
79	  bijective : Function.Bijective (contractLeft R M)
80	
81	namespace Invertible
82	
83	/-- Promote the canonical map `Mᵛ ⊗[R] M → R` to a linear equivalence for invertible `M`. -/
84	noncomputable def linearEquiv [Module.Invertible R M] : Module.Dual R M ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[R] R :=
85	  .ofBijective _ Invertible.bijective
86	
87	variable {R M N}
88	
89	section LinearEquiv
90	
91	variable (e : M ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] R)
92	
93	/-- The canonical isomorphism between a module and the result of tensoring it
94	from the left by two mutually dual invertible modules. -/
95	noncomputable abbrev leftCancelEquiv : M ⊗[R] (N ⊗[R] P) ≃ₗ[R] P :=
96	  (TensorProduct.assoc R M N P).symm ≪≫ₗ e.rTensor P ≪≫ₗ TensorProduct.lid R P
97	
98	/-- The canonical isomorphism between a module and the result of tensoring it
99	from the right by two mutually dual invertible modules. -/
100	noncomputable abbrev rightCancelEquiv : (P ⊗[R] M) ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] P :=
101	  TensorProduct.assoc R P M N ≪≫ₗ e.lTensor P ≪≫ₗ TensorProduct.rid R P
102	
103	variable {P Q} in
104	theorem leftCancelEquiv_comp_lTensor_comp_symm (f : P →ₗ[R] Q) :
105	    leftCancelEquiv Q e ∘ₗ (f.lTensor N).lTensor M ∘ₗ (leftCancelEquiv P e).symm = f := by
106	  rw [← LinearMap.comp_assoc, LinearEquiv.comp_toLinearMap_symm_eq]; ext; simp
107	
108	variable {P Q} in
109	theorem rightCancelEquiv_comp_rTensor_comp_symm (f : P →ₗ[R] Q) :
110	    rightCancelEquiv Q e ∘ₗ (f.rTensor M).rTensor N ∘ₗ (rightCancelEquiv P e).symm = f := by
111	  rw [← LinearMap.comp_assoc, LinearEquiv.comp_toLinearMap_symm_eq]; ext; simp
112	
113	/-- If M is invertible, `rTensorHom M` admits an inverse. -/
114	noncomputable def rTensorInv : (P ⊗[R] M →ₗ[R] Q ⊗[R] M) →ₗ[R] (P →ₗ[R] Q) :=
115	  ((rightCancelEquiv Q e).congrRight ≪≫ₗ (rightCancelEquiv P e).congrLeft _ R) ∘ₗ
116	    LinearMap.rTensorHom N
117	
118	theorem rTensorInv_leftInverse : Function.LeftInverse (rTensorInv P Q e) (.rTensorHom M) :=
119	  fun _ ↦ by
120	    simp_rw [rTensorInv, LinearEquiv.coe_trans, LinearMap.comp_apply, LinearEquiv.coe_toLinearMap]

26	- `Module.Invertible R M` says that the canonical map `Mᵛ ⊗[R] M → R` is an isomorphism.
27	  To show that `M` is invertible, it suffices to provide an arbitrary `R`-module `N`
28	  and an isomorphism `N ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[R] R`, see `Module.Invertible.right`.
29	
30	- `ClassGroup.equivPic`: the class group of a domain is isomorphic to the Picard group.
31	
32	## Main results
33	
34	- An invertible module is finite and projective (provided as instances).
35	
36	- `Module.Invertible.free_iff_linearEquiv`: an invertible module is free iff it is isomorphic to
37	  the ring, i.e. its class is trivial in the Picard group.
38	
39	- `Submodule.ker_unitsToPic`, `Submodule.range_unitsToPic`: exactness of the sequence
40	  `1 → Rˣ → Aˣ → (Submodule R A)ˣ → Pic R → Pic A` at the last two spots.
41	  See Theorem 2.4 in [RobertsSingh1993] or Exercise I.3.7(iv) and Proposition I.3.5 in [Weibel2013].
42

48	- [Weibel2013], https://sites.math.rutgers.edu/~weibel/Kbook/Kbook.I.pdf
49	- [Stacks: Picard groups of rings](https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0AFW)

99	from the right by two mutually dual invertible modules. -/
100	noncomputable abbrev rightCancelEquiv : (P ⊗[R] M) ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] P :=
101	  TensorProduct.assoc R P M N ≪≫ₗ e.lTensor P ≪≫ₗ TensorProduct.rid R P
102	
103	variable {P Q} in
104	theorem leftCancelEquiv_comp_lTensor_comp_symm (f : P →ₗ[R] Q) :
105	    leftCancelEquiv Q e ∘ₗ (f.lTensor N).lTensor M ∘ₗ (leftCancelEquiv P e).symm = f := by
106	  rw [← LinearMap.comp_assoc, LinearEquiv.comp_toLinearMap_symm_eq]; ext; simp
107	
108	variable {P Q} in
109	theorem rightCancelEquiv_comp_rTensor_comp_symm (f : P →ₗ[R] Q) :
110	    rightCancelEquiv Q e ∘ₗ (f.rTensor M).rTensor N ∘ₗ (rightCancelEquiv P e).symm = f := by
111	  rw [← LinearMap.comp_assoc, LinearEquiv.comp_toLinearMap_symm_eq]; ext; simp
112	
113	/-- If M is invertible, `rTensorHom M` admits an inverse. -/
114	noncomputable def rTensorInv : (P ⊗[R] M →ₗ[R] Q ⊗[R] M) →ₗ[R] (P →ₗ[R] Q) :=
115	  ((rightCancelEquiv Q e).congrRight ≪≫ₗ (rightCancelEquiv P e).congrLeft _ R) ∘ₗ
116	    LinearMap.rTensorHom N
117	
118	theorem rTensorInv_leftInverse : Function.LeftInverse (rTensorInv P Q e) (.rTensorHom M) :=
119	  fun _ ↦ by
120	    simp_rw [rTensorInv, LinearEquiv.coe_trans, LinearMap.comp_apply, LinearEquiv.coe_toLinearMap]
121	    rw [← LinearEquiv.eq_symm_apply]
122	    ext; simp [LinearEquiv.congrLeft, LinearEquiv.congrRight, LinearEquiv.arrowCongrAddEquiv]
123	
124	theorem rTensorInv_injective : Function.Injective (rTensorInv P Q e) := by
125	  simpa [rTensorInv] using (rTensorInv_leftInverse _ _ <| TensorProduct.comm R N M ≪≫ₗ e).injective
126	
127	/-- If `M` is an invertible `R`-module, `(· ⊗[R] M)` is an auto-equivalence of the category
128	of `R`-modules. -/
129	@[simps!] noncomputable def rTensorEquiv : (P →ₗ[R] Q) ≃ₗ[R] (P ⊗[R] M →ₗ[R] Q ⊗[R] M) where
130	  __ := LinearMap.rTensorHom M
131	  invFun := rTensorInv P Q e
132	  left_inv := rTensorInv_leftInverse P Q e
133	  right_inv _ := rTensorInv_injective P Q e (by rw [LinearMap.toFun_eq_coe, rTensorInv_leftInverse])
134	
135	open LinearMap in
136	/-- If there is an `R`-isomorphism between `M ⊗[R] N` and `R`,
137	the induced map `M → Nᵛ` is an isomorphism. -/
138	theorem bijective_curry : Function.Bijective (curry e.toLinearMap) := by
139	  have : curry e.toLinearMap = ((TensorProduct.lid R N).congrLeft _ R ≪≫ₗ e.congrRight) ∘ₗ
140	      rTensorHom N ∘ₗ (ringLmapEquivSelf R R M).symm.toLinearMap := by
141	    rw [← LinearEquiv.toLinearMap_symm_comp_eq]; ext
142	    simp [LinearEquiv.congrLeft, LinearEquiv.congrRight, LinearEquiv.arrowCongrAddEquiv]
143	  simpa [this] using (rTensorEquiv R M <| TensorProduct.comm R N M ≪≫ₗ e).bijective
144	
145	/-- Given `M ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] R`, this is the induced isomorphism `M ≃ₗ[R] Nᵛ`. -/
146	noncomputable def linearEquivDual : M ≃ₗ[R] Dual R N := .ofBijective _ (bijective_curry e)
147	
148	include e
149	
150	protected theorem right : Module.Invertible R N where
151	  bijective := by
152	    rw [show contractLeft R N = ((linearEquivDual e).rTensor N).symm ≪≫ₗ e by
153	      rw [LinearEquiv.coe_trans, LinearEquiv.eq_comp_toLinearMap_symm]; ext; rfl]
154	    apply LinearEquiv.bijective
155	
156	protected theorem left : Module.Invertible R M := .right (TensorProduct.comm R N M ≪≫ₗ e)
157

70	variable (R : Type u) (M : Type v) (N P Q A : Type*) [CommSemiring R]

74	namespace Module

76	/-- An `R`-module `M` is invertible if the canonical map `Mᵛ ⊗[R] M → R` is an isomorphism,
77	where `Mᵛ` is the `R`-dual of `M`. -/

79	  bijective : Function.Bijective (contractLeft R M)

83	/-- Promote the canonical map `Mᵛ ⊗[R] M → R` to a linear equivalence for invertible `M`. -/
84	noncomputable def linearEquiv [Module.Invertible R M] : Module.Dual R M ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[R] R :=
85	  .ofBijective _ Invertible.bijective
