90	
91	variable (e : M ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] R)
92	
93	/-- The canonical isomorphism between a module and the result of tensoring it
94	from the left by two mutually dual invertible modules. -/
95	noncomputable abbrev leftCancelEquiv : M ⊗[R] (N ⊗[R] P) ≃ₗ[R] P :=
96	  (TensorProduct.assoc R M N P).symm ≪≫ₗ e.rTensor P ≪≫ₗ TensorProduct.lid R P
97	
98	/-- The canonical isomorphism between a module and the result of tensoring it
99	from the right by two mutually dual invertible modules. -/
100	noncomputable abbrev rightCancelEquiv : (P ⊗[R] M) ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] P :=
101	  TensorProduct.assoc R P M N ≪≫ₗ e.lTensor P ≪≫ₗ TensorProduct.rid R P
102	
103	variable {P Q} in
104	theorem leftCancelEquiv_comp_lTensor_comp_symm (f : P →ₗ[R] Q) :
105	    leftCancelEquiv Q e ∘ₗ (f.lTensor N).lTensor M ∘ₗ (leftCancelEquiv P e).symm = f := by
106	  rw [← LinearMap.comp_assoc, LinearEquiv.comp_toLinearMap_symm_eq]; ext; simp
107	
108	variable {P Q} in
109	theorem rightCancelEquiv_comp_rTensor_comp_symm (f : P →ₗ[R] Q) :
110	    rightCancelEquiv Q e ∘ₗ (f.rTensor M).rTensor N ∘ₗ (rightCancelEquiv P e).symm = f := by
111	  rw [← LinearMap.comp_assoc, LinearEquiv.comp_toLinearMap_symm_eq]; ext; simp
112	
113	/-- If M is invertible, `rTensorHom M` admits an inverse. -/
114	noncomputable def rTensorInv : (P ⊗[R] M →ₗ[R] Q ⊗[R] M) →ₗ[R] (P →ₗ[R] Q) :=
115	  ((rightCancelEquiv Q e).congrRight ≪≫ₗ (rightCancelEquiv P e).congrLeft _ R) ∘ₗ
116	    LinearMap.rTensorHom N
117	
118	theorem rTensorInv_leftInverse : Function.LeftInverse (rTensorInv P Q e) (.rTensorHom M) :=
119	  fun _ ↦ by
120	    simp_rw [rTensorInv, LinearEquiv.coe_trans, LinearMap.comp_apply, LinearEquiv.coe_toLinearMap]
121	    rw [← LinearEquiv.eq_symm_apply]
122	    ext; simp [LinearEquiv.congrLeft, LinearEquiv.congrRight, LinearEquiv.arrowCongrAddEquiv]
123	
124	theorem rTensorInv_injective : Function.Injective (rTensorInv P Q e) := by
125	  simpa [rTensorInv] using (rTensorInv_leftInverse _ _ <| TensorProduct.comm R N M ≪≫ₗ e).injective
126	
127	/-- If `M` is an invertible `R`-module, `(· ⊗[R] M)` is an auto-equivalence of the category
128	of `R`-modules. -/
129	@[simps!] noncomputable def rTensorEquiv : (P →ₗ[R] Q) ≃ₗ[R] (P ⊗[R] M →ₗ[R] Q ⊗[R] M) where
130	  __ := LinearMap.rTensorHom M
131	  invFun := rTensorInv P Q e
132	  left_inv := rTensorInv_leftInverse P Q e
133	  right_inv _ := rTensorInv_injective P Q e (by rw [LinearMap.toFun_eq_coe, rTensorInv_leftInverse])
134	
135	open LinearMap in
136	/-- If there is an `R`-isomorphism between `M ⊗[R] N` and `R`,
137	the induced map `M → Nᵛ` is an isomorphism. -/
138	theorem bijective_curry : Function.Bijective (curry e.toLinearMap) := by
139	  have : curry e.toLinearMap = ((TensorProduct.lid R N).congrLeft _ R ≪≫ₗ e.congrRight) ∘ₗ
140	      rTensorHom N ∘ₗ (ringLmapEquivSelf R R M).symm.toLinearMap := by
141	    rw [← LinearEquiv.toLinearMap_symm_comp_eq]; ext
142	    simp [LinearEquiv.congrLeft, LinearEquiv.congrRight, LinearEquiv.arrowCongrAddEquiv]
143	  simpa [this] using (rTensorEquiv R M <| TensorProduct.comm R N M ≪≫ₗ e).bijective
144	
145	/-- Given `M ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] R`, this is the induced isomorphism `M ≃ₗ[R] Nᵛ`. -/
146	noncomputable def linearEquivDual : M ≃ₗ[R] Dual R N := .ofBijective _ (bijective_curry e)
147	
148	include e
149	
150	protected theorem right : Module.Invertible R N where
151	  bijective := by
152	    rw [show contractLeft R N = ((linearEquivDual e).rTensor N).symm ≪≫ₗ e by
153	      rw [LinearEquiv.coe_trans, LinearEquiv.eq_comp_toLinearMap_symm]; ext; rfl]
154	    apply LinearEquiv.bijective
155	
156	protected theorem left : Module.Invertible R M := .right (TensorProduct.comm R N M ≪≫ₗ e)
157	
158	instance : Module.Invertible R R := .left (TensorProduct.lid R R)
159	
160	end LinearEquiv
161	
162	variable [Module.Invertible R M]
163	
164	protected theorem congr (e : M ≃ₗ[R] N) : Module.Invertible R N :=
165	  .right (e.symm.lTensor _ ≪≫ₗ linearEquiv R M)
166	
167	variable (R M N)
168	
169	instance : Module.Invertible R (Dual R M) := .left (linearEquiv R M)
170	
171	instance [Module.Invertible R N] : Module.Invertible R (M ⊗[R] N) :=
172	  .right (M := Dual R M ⊗[R] Dual R N) <| tensorTensorTensorComm .. ≪≫ₗ
173	    congr (linearEquiv R M) (linearEquiv R N) ≪≫ₗ TensorProduct.lid R R
174	
175	private theorem finite_projective : Module.Finite R M ∧ Projective R M := by
176	  let N := Dual R M
177	  let e : M ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] R := TensorProduct.comm .. ≪≫ₗ linearEquiv R M
178	  have ⟨S, hS⟩ := TensorProduct.exists_finset (e.symm 1)
179	  let f : (S →₀ N) →ₗ[R] R := Finsupp.lsum R fun i ↦ e.toLinearMap ∘ₗ TensorProduct.mk R M N i.1.1
180	  have : Function.Surjective f := by
181	    rw [← LinearMap.range_eq_top, Ideal.eq_top_iff_one]
182	    use Finsupp.equivFunOnFinite.symm fun i ↦ i.1.2
183	    simp_rw [f, Finsupp.coe_lsum]
184	    rw [Finsupp.sum_fintype _ _ fun _ ↦ map_zero _]
185	    rwa [e.symm_apply_eq, map_sum, ← Finset.sum_coe_sort, eq_comm] at hS
186	  have ⟨g, hg⟩ := projective_lifting_property f .id this
187	  classical
188	  let aux := finsuppRight R _ M N S ≪≫ₗ Finsupp.mapRange.linearEquiv e
189	  let f' : (S →₀ R) →ₗ[R] M := TensorProduct.rid R M ∘ₗ f.lTensor M ∘ₗ aux.symm
190	  let g' : M →ₗ[R] S →₀ R := aux ∘ₗ g.lTensor M ∘ₗ (TensorProduct.rid R M).symm
191	  have : Function.Surjective f' := by simpa [f'] using LinearMap.lTensor_surjective _ this
192	  refine ⟨.of_surjective f' this, .of_split g' f' <| LinearMap.ext fun m ↦ ?_⟩
193	  simp [f', g', show f (g 1) = 1 from DFunLike.congr_fun hg 1]
194	
195	instance : Module.Finite R M := (finite_projective R M).1
196	instance : Projective R M := (finite_projective R M).2
197	example : IsReflexive R M := inferInstance
198	
199	section inj_surj_bij

203	theorem lTensor_injective_iff {f : N →ₗ[R] P} :
204	    Function.Injective (f.lTensor M) ↔ Function.Injective f := by
205	  refine ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, Flat.lTensor_preserves_injective_linearMap _⟩

66	open TensorProduct

68	universe u v
69	
70	variable (R : Type u) (M : Type v) (N P Q A : Type*) [CommSemiring R]
71	variable [AddCommMonoid M] [AddCommMonoid N] [AddCommMonoid P] [AddCommMonoid Q]
72	variable [Module R M] [Module R N] [Module R P] [Module R Q]
73	
74	namespace Module
75	
76	/-- An `R`-module `M` is invertible if the canonical map `Mᵛ ⊗[R] M → R` is an isomorphism,
77	where `Mᵛ` is the `R`-dual of `M`. -/
78	protected class Invertible : Prop where
79	  bijective : Function.Bijective (contractLeft R M)
80	
81	namespace Invertible
82	
83	/-- Promote the canonical map `Mᵛ ⊗[R] M → R` to a linear equivalence for invertible `M`. -/
84	noncomputable def linearEquiv [Module.Invertible R M] : Module.Dual R M ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[R] R :=
85	  .ofBijective _ Invertible.bijective
86	
87	variable {R M N}
88	
89	section LinearEquiv
90	
91	variable (e : M ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] R)
92	
93	/-- The canonical isomorphism between a module and the result of tensoring it
94	from the left by two mutually dual invertible modules. -/
95	noncomputable abbrev leftCancelEquiv : M ⊗[R] (N ⊗[R] P) ≃ₗ[R] P :=
96	  (TensorProduct.assoc R M N P).symm ≪≫ₗ e.rTensor P ≪≫ₗ TensorProduct.lid R P
97	
98	/-- The canonical isomorphism between a module and the result of tensoring it
99	from the right by two mutually dual invertible modules. -/
100	noncomputable abbrev rightCancelEquiv : (P ⊗[R] M) ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] P :=
101	  TensorProduct.assoc R P M N ≪≫ₗ e.lTensor P ≪≫ₗ TensorProduct.rid R P
102	
103	variable {P Q} in
104	theorem leftCancelEquiv_comp_lTensor_comp_symm (f : P →ₗ[R] Q) :
105	    leftCancelEquiv Q e ∘ₗ (f.lTensor N).lTensor M ∘ₗ (leftCancelEquiv P e).symm = f := by
106	  rw [← LinearMap.comp_assoc, LinearEquiv.comp_toLinearMap_symm_eq]; ext; simp
107	
108	variable {P Q} in
109	theorem rightCancelEquiv_comp_rTensor_comp_symm (f : P →ₗ[R] Q) :
110	    rightCancelEquiv Q e ∘ₗ (f.rTensor M).rTensor N ∘ₗ (rightCancelEquiv P e).symm = f := by
111	  rw [← LinearMap.comp_assoc, LinearEquiv.comp_toLinearMap_symm_eq]; ext; simp
112	
113	/-- If M is invertible, `rTensorHom M` admits an inverse. -/
114	noncomputable def rTensorInv : (P ⊗[R] M →ₗ[R] Q ⊗[R] M) →ₗ[R] (P →ₗ[R] Q) :=
115	  ((rightCancelEquiv Q e).congrRight ≪≫ₗ (rightCancelEquiv P e).congrLeft _ R) ∘ₗ
116	    LinearMap.rTensorHom N
117	
118	theorem rTensorInv_leftInverse : Function.LeftInverse (rTensorInv P Q e) (.rTensorHom M) :=
119	  fun _ ↦ by
120	    simp_rw [rTensorInv, LinearEquiv.coe_trans, LinearMap.comp_apply, LinearEquiv.coe_toLinearMap]
121	    rw [← LinearEquiv.eq_symm_apply]
122	    ext; simp [LinearEquiv.congrLeft, LinearEquiv.congrRight, LinearEquiv.arrowCongrAddEquiv]
123	
124	theorem rTensorInv_injective : Function.Injective (rTensorInv P Q e) := by
125	  simpa [rTensorInv] using (rTensorInv_leftInverse _ _ <| TensorProduct.comm R N M ≪≫ₗ e).injective
126	
127	/-- If `M` is an invertible `R`-module, `(· ⊗[R] M)` is an auto-equivalence of the category
128	of `R`-modules. -/
129	@[simps!] noncomputable def rTensorEquiv : (P →ₗ[R] Q) ≃ₗ[R] (P ⊗[R] M →ₗ[R] Q ⊗[R] M) where
130	  __ := LinearMap.rTensorHom M
131	  invFun := rTensorInv P Q e
132	  left_inv := rTensorInv_leftInverse P Q e
133	  right_inv _ := rTensorInv_injective P Q e (by rw [LinearMap.toFun_eq_coe, rTensorInv_leftInverse])
134	
135	open LinearMap in
136	/-- If there is an `R`-isomorphism between `M ⊗[R] N` and `R`,
137	the induced map `M → Nᵛ` is an isomorphism. -/
138	theorem bijective_curry : Function.Bijective (curry e.toLinearMap) := by
139	  have : curry e.toLinearMap = ((TensorProduct.lid R N).congrLeft _ R ≪≫ₗ e.congrRight) ∘ₗ
140	      rTensorHom N ∘ₗ (ringLmapEquivSelf R R M).symm.toLinearMap := by
141	    rw [← LinearEquiv.toLinearMap_symm_comp_eq]; ext
142	    simp [LinearEquiv.congrLeft, LinearEquiv.congrRight, LinearEquiv.arrowCongrAddEquiv]
143	  simpa [this] using (rTensorEquiv R M <| TensorProduct.comm R N M ≪≫ₗ e).bijective
144	
145	/-- Given `M ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] R`, this is the induced isomorphism `M ≃ₗ[R] Nᵛ`. -/
146	noncomputable def linearEquivDual : M ≃ₗ[R] Dual R N := .ofBijective _ (bijective_curry e)
147	
148	include e
149	
150	protected theorem right : Module.Invertible R N where
151	  bijective := by
152	    rw [show contractLeft R N = ((linearEquivDual e).rTensor N).symm ≪≫ₗ e by
153	      rw [LinearEquiv.coe_trans, LinearEquiv.eq_comp_toLinearMap_symm]; ext; rfl]
154	    apply LinearEquiv.bijective
155	
156	protected theorem left : Module.Invertible R M := .right (TensorProduct.comm R N M ≪≫ₗ e)
157	
158	instance : Module.Invertible R R := .left (TensorProduct.lid R R)
159	
160	end LinearEquiv

162	variable [Module.Invertible R M]
163	
164	protected theorem congr (e : M ≃ₗ[R] N) : Module.Invertible R N :=
165	  .right (e.symm.lTensor _ ≪≫ₗ linearEquiv R M)
166	
167	variable (R M N)
168	
169	instance : Module.Invertible R (Dual R M) := .left (linearEquiv R M)
170	
171	instance [Module.Invertible R N] : Module.Invertible R (M ⊗[R] N) :=
172	  .right (M := Dual R M ⊗[R] Dual R N) <| tensorTensorTensorComm .. ≪≫ₗ
173	    congr (linearEquiv R M) (linearEquiv R N) ≪≫ₗ TensorProduct.lid R R
174	
175	private theorem finite_projective : Module.Finite R M ∧ Projective R M := by
176	  let N := Dual R M
177	  let e : M ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] R := TensorProduct.comm .. ≪≫ₗ linearEquiv R M
178	  have ⟨S, hS⟩ := TensorProduct.exists_finset (e.symm 1)
179	  let f : (S →₀ N) →ₗ[R] R := Finsupp.lsum R fun i ↦ e.toLinearMap ∘ₗ TensorProduct.mk R M N i.1.1
180	  have : Function.Surjective f := by
181	    rw [← LinearMap.range_eq_top, Ideal.eq_top_iff_one]
182	    use Finsupp.equivFunOnFinite.symm fun i ↦ i.1.2
183	    simp_rw [f, Finsupp.coe_lsum]
184	    rw [Finsupp.sum_fintype _ _ fun _ ↦ map_zero _]
185	    rwa [e.symm_apply_eq, map_sum, ← Finset.sum_coe_sort, eq_comm] at hS
186	  have ⟨g, hg⟩ := projective_lifting_property f .id this
187	  classical
188	  let aux := finsuppRight R _ M N S ≪≫ₗ Finsupp.mapRange.linearEquiv e
189	  let f' : (S →₀ R) →ₗ[R] M := TensorProduct.rid R M ∘ₗ f.lTensor M ∘ₗ aux.symm
190	  let g' : M →ₗ[R] S →₀ R := aux ∘ₗ g.lTensor M ∘ₗ (TensorProduct.rid R M).symm
191	  have : Function.Surjective f' := by simpa [f'] using LinearMap.lTensor_surjective _ this
192	  refine ⟨.of_surjective f' this, .of_split g' f' <| LinearMap.ext fun m ↦ ?_⟩
193	  simp [f', g', show f (g 1) = 1 from DFunLike.congr_fun hg 1]
194	
195	instance : Module.Finite R M := (finite_projective R M).1
196	instance : Projective R M := (finite_projective R M).2
197	example : IsReflexive R M := inferInstance

105	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not

107	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so

115	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise

117	assembles the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units group is the

119	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) as

input.new_string:
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
existence-of-isomorphism. This is \emph{not} the route by which Mathlib builds the
fixed-ring Picard group: there \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) is defined as
\(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\), with its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\)
read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the \emph{coherent} monoidal category
\(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that Mathlib supplies
over a fixed ring (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}). The present construction
\emph{cannot} take that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, precisely because the coherent
\(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\) on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the
\emph{varying} structure sheaf is exactly what it avoids; hence the by-hand assembly.
The \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk apparatus is
therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.

of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
Mathlib's own Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\)
(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}). The two constructions are obtained

\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) and

monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)

route --- the coherent \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\) on
\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what is
unavailable --- so its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built by hand from the three
existence-of-isomorphism facts above.

  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, because the coherent \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\) on
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) it would require is exactly what is unavailable for
  the varying structure sheaf; the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is instead built by hand
  from four existence-of-isomorphism facts.}

  them yields the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\), à la \(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\)
  / \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) in Mathlib but specialized to the \(\otimes\)-invertible

input.new_string:
These four existence facts are exactly the group axioms on iso-classes; packaging
  them directly --- defining the \(\mathtt{mul\_assoc}\), \(\mathtt{one\_mul}\) /
  \(\mathtt{mul\_one}\), \(\mathtt{mul\_comm}\) and inverse fields each from a single
  existence-of-isomorphism --- yields the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\). We do \emph{not} go
  through \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or
  \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\): those require a coherent
  \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier
  category, i.e.\ the coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
  that this construction explicitly avoids. Mathlib's fixed-ring
  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R = \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) can
  afford that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route because it builds
  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring; here
  no such coherent monoidal category is available, so the group is assembled by hand,
  exactly as \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) builds its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) directly on
  invertible-module iso-classes rather than on a closed or otherwise enriched
  structure.

The associator construction consumes the restriction-compatibility linchpin
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} but \emph{not} \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\):
the gluing is direct. This is the substrate's critical-path realization of the
associativity the group law needs.
input.new_string:
The associator construction consumes the restriction-compatibility linchpin
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} but \emph{not} \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\):
the gluing is direct. This is the substrate's critical-path realization of the
associativity the group law needs.

\emph{The free-cover make-or-break.} The group law of
\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} consumes the associator only for
\emph{locally-trivial} modules (line bundles), and the cover
\(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) above is chosen to \emph{trivialise} them: on each \(U_i\)
the modules \(M_i, N_i, P_i\) are \emph{free} \(A_i\)-modules. This is the entire
point of building the associator by gluing rather than by instantiating a coherent
monoidal category. On a free cover the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} that the gluing consumes is needed \emph{only} in
the cheap, structural case: where each module is free the sheaf-level tensor
\(\otimes_X\) coincides on \(U_i\) with the presheaf-level tensor (sheafification is
the identity on the already-sheafy free pieces), so the comparison
\((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i} \to M|_{U_i} \otimes_{U_i} N|_{U_i}\) is the elementary
base-change isomorphism of free modules along the open immersion, requiring no
general presheaf-pushforward adjunction. The PRIMARY proof obligation of the whole
construction is therefore exactly this: restriction-compatibility \emph{on the free
cover}, established \emph{without} the Mathlib-absent general adjunction
\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) (``H1'', see
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). As a fallback, \emph{if} the free-cover
restriction-compatibility genuinely cannot be shown without that general adjunction
(H1), then the by-hand construction has not in fact gotten smaller, and one would
instead obtain associativity by comparing against the superseded route~(e)
\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \(\to\) \(\mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
(\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}); that route remains off-path unless this free-cover
obligation forces it back.

input.new_string:
This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law: the
  direct gluing construction of the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
  invokes it (twice per cover member) to descend to the canonical presheaf-level
  associator. Closing the H1 residual is therefore the sole remaining substrate
  obligation.

  \emph{The make-or-break: only the free-cover case is on the critical path.} The
  statement above is proved here for \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
  \(M, N\), and in that generality the closure passes through the H1 residual
  (\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)), whose
  presheaf-level building blocks are Mathlib-absent. But the associator
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes this comparison only on a \emph{trivialising
  cover}, where \(M\) and \(N\) restrict to \emph{free} modules. On that free cover
  the comparison is the elementary, structural one: sheaf-\(\otimes\) coincides with
  presheaf-\(\otimes\) on the free pieces, and restriction along the open immersion is
  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{ring isomorphism} \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\),
  so the isomorphism follows from
  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} alone, with \emph{no} appeal to the
  general presheaf-pushforward adjunction. The PRIMARY proof obligation of the
  construction is therefore the free-cover specialisation of this lemma, to be
  discharged \emph{without} H1; the arbitrary-module statement (with its H1 residual)
  is strictly stronger than the group law requires. As a fallback, should the
  free-cover case itself genuinely require the general adjunction H1, the by-hand
  route would have gained nothing over instantiating the coherent monoidal structure,
  and one would revert to comparing against the superseded route~(e)
  \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}).
\end{proof}

This section records the
\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
to the off-path route~(e).

# Blueprint Writer Report

## Slug
ts216c

## Status
COMPLETE — all three directive items applied; one correctness caveat in the
directive's own rationale was found against the Mathlib source and resolved by
taking the directive-permitted "contrast" framing (see Strategy-modifying findings).

## Target chapter
blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex

## Changes Made

### Item 1 — CORRECTNESS FIX of the `monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal` / `Skeleton` mis-citation
Applied consistently at the three places the chapter framed the group law as "à la
`Skeleton`":

- **Revised `lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup` source block** (was a bare
  `\textit{Source: ... built as Units(Skeleton(ModuleCat R))}`). Replaced with an
  accurate `% SOURCE:` + verbatim `% SOURCE QUOTE:` from
  `Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean` (L28–L44, opened this session) plus a
  reframed `\textit{Source: ...}`: this is an Archon-original **by-hand** packaging;
  the fixed-ring precedent `CommRing.Pic R := Skeleton(Module.Invertible R)` reads its
  `CommGroup` off the `Skeleton` of the coherent `MonoidalCategory(Module.Invertible R)`
  Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring, and the present construction does **not** take
  that route because the coherent `MonoidalCategory` on `X.Modules` it would need is
  exactly what is unavailable for the varying structure sheaf.
- **Revised `lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup` proof tail** — removed "à la
  `monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal` / `Skeleton` … specialized to the ⊗-invertible classes".
  Now states the four group-axiom fields (`mul_assoc`, `one_mul`/`mul_one`,
  `mul_comm`, inverse) are each defined directly from a single existence-of-iso, and
  explicitly that we do **not** go through `CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal` /
  `Skeleton` because those require a coherent `[MonoidalCategory]` (and
  `[BraidedCategory]`) on the carrier — the very structure on `X.Modules` this
  construction avoids — kept only as a contrast.
- **Revised motivation passage (§ motivation, ~L116–122)** — was "exactly as Mathlib
  builds `CommRing.Pic` as `Units(Skeleton(ModuleCat R))`". Reframed to by-hand
  assembly with the accurate `Skeleton(Module.Invertible R)` contrast.
- **Revised off-path remark `rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path` (~L409–414)** — was
  "This mirrors Mathlib's own `CommRing.Pic` … transported from
  `Units(Skeleton(ModuleCat R))`". Reframed: same carrier (scheme analogue of
  `CommRing.Pic`), but the two are built differently — Mathlib via `Skeleton` of the
  coherent monoidal `Module.Invertible R`, here by hand because that coherent monoidal
  category is unavailable.

### Item 2 — FREE-COVER make-or-break (added to both `lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso` and `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`)
- **`lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso`** — added an `\emph{The free-cover make-or-break.}`
  paragraph after the lemma: the group law consumes the associator only for
  locally-trivial (line) bundles, the gluing cover trivialises them to **free**
  modules, and on a free cover the restriction-compatibility `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`
  reduces to the elementary base-change of free modules along the open immersion
  (sheaf-⊗ = presheaf-⊗ on free pieces), needing **no** general presheaf-pushforward
  adjunction. States the PRIMARY obligation = restriction-compatibility on the free
  cover without H1; fallback = if it forces H1, no size reduction, revert to comparing
  against route-(e) `(J.W).IsMonoidal → Sheaf.monoidalCategory` (`lem:jw_ismonoidal`).
- **`lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`** — added an
  `\emph{The make-or-break: only the free-cover case is on the critical path.}`
  paragraph to the proof's closing: the arbitrary-module statement carries the H1
  residual, but the associator consumes it only on the trivialising free cover, where
  it follows from `lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv` alone (base change along
  the structure-sheaf ring iso `f.appIso`), no H1. PRIMARY obligation = free-cover
  specialisation without H1; same route-(e) fallback noted. `lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv`
  left untouched as required.

### Item 3 — route-(e) demotion completion
- **Revised `sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift` intro (~L427)** — removed the live-source
  framing "under route~(e) these are read off the `MonoidalCategory` structure of
  `lem:jw_ismonoidal`". Now: the existence facts are supplied **directly** (assoc by
  gluing via `restrict_iso`, unit/comm by `mapIso`, inverse by ⊗-invertibility), and
  the route deriving them from `lem:jw_ismonoidal` is superseded/off-path. Also fixed
  the contradictory final sentence: `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso` is on the critical
  path because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
  `lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso`, **not** by the off-path `lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W`.
- **`lem:jw_ismonoidal` block (~L1127–1166)** and **chapter summary
  (`sec:tensorobj_summary`, ~L1945–1971)** — inspected; already fully demoted by a
  prior pass ("Superseded route --- off path", "retained only for the superseded
  route~(e)", "Off the critical path (superseded route~(e) / stalk routes)"). No live
  promotion remained, so no further edit was required there. Labels left resolvable.

## Cross-references introduced
- No new `\uses{}` edges. New prose references existing labels
  (`lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso`, `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`,
  `lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv`, `lem:tensorobj_unit_iso`,
  `lem:tensorobj_comm_iso`, `lem:jw_ismonoidal`,
  `lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W`, `def:scheme_modules_isinvertible`) — all
  present in this chapter.

## References consulted
- `.lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean` (L1–L44) — verbatim
  source for the corrected `CommRing.Pic` / `Module.Invertible` / `CommGroup (Pic R)`
  citation in `lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup`. This is the file that revealed the
  directive's rationale error (see below).

## Macros needed (if any)
- None. Used only existing macros (`\Scheme`, `\MonoidalCategory`, `\Spec`, `\mathtt`,
  `\cref`). `\MonoidalCategory` was already in use in the chapter.

## Notes for Plan Agent
- The directive's item 1 said to cite `CommRing.Pic` / `instCommGroupPic` as a
  precedent that "builds a `CommGroup` directly on `Module.Invertible` iso-classes
  WITHOUT constructing a monoidal category." **The Mathlib source contradicts this.**
  `Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean` defines
  `CommRing.Pic R := Skeleton (Module.Invertible R)` with
  `instance : CommGroup (Pic R) := inferInstanceAs <| CommGroup <| Skeleton _`, and it
  **does** build `instance : MonoidalCategory (Module.Invertible R)` (L42–44). So
  `CommRing.Pic` is itself the `Skeleton`/`monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal` route over a fixed
  ring; it does construct a (coherent, monoidal) category and take its `Skeleton`.
- The directive's deeper, **correct** point still holds: this chapter's construction
  CANNOT be "à la `Skeleton`" because `Skeleton`/`monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal` require a
  coherent `MonoidalCategory` on the carrier — here `X.Modules` for the *varying*
  structure sheaf — which is exactly what the chapter avoids. I therefore took the
  directive's explicitly-permitted alternative ("keep it ONLY as a contrast: we do not
  use `Skeleton`, which would require the coherent `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)`").
  This is both directive-permitted and faithful to the source. I did **not** write the
  source-contradicting claim that `CommRing.Pic` avoids a monoidal category.
- There is no `instCommGroupPic` declaration by that name in the file; the instance is
  the anonymous `instance : CommGroup (Pic R)`. I cited it by its actual form.
- The garbled line-numbering seen while reading (duplicated headers around the
  commgroup lemma) was a Read-display artifact; a clean re-read confirmed the LaTeX is
  well-formed (stub `commgroup_intro` lemma + the real `commgroup` lemma, no duplicate
  optional-arg brackets).

## Strategy-modifying findings
- Minor / citation-level, not strategy-level: the precedent `CommRing.Pic` is **not**
  an instance of the by-hand-without-a-monoidal-category pattern (it is the
  `Skeleton`-of-a-monoidal-category pattern). The chapter's strategy — build the
  `CommGroup` by hand from three existence-of-iso facts because no coherent
  `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` is available — is unaffected and is, if anything,
  sharpened by the contrast. No STRATEGY.md change is required, but the plan agent
  should not re-introduce the "CommRing.Pic builds a group without a monoidal
  category" phrasing in future directives.

\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}

The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
to the off-path route~(e).

1252	\begin{proof}
1253	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1254	  The isomorphism is assembled by \emph{gluing canonical local isomorphisms}. The
1255	  construction rests on \emph{no} whiskering of the sheafification localizer,
1256	  \emph{no} instance \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and \emph{no} stalk
1257	  computation.
1258	
1259	  Fix a common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\) (for instance an affine open cover).
1260	  On each member \(U\) form the canonical composite
1261	  \[
1262	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1263	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1264	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1265	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1266	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1267	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1268	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1269	  \]
1270	  in which:
1271	  \begin{itemize}
1272	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1273	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1274	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1275	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1276	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1277	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1278	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1279	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1280	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1281	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1282	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1283	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1284	  \end{itemize}
1285	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1286	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1287	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1288	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1289	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1290	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1291	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1292	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1293	  composites agree on the overlap.

1295	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1296	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1297	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single

1301	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from

1310	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1311	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1312	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the

  associator by gluing rather than by instantiating a coherent monoidal category. On a
  free cover the two applications of the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} that each local composite consumes are needed
  \emph{only} in the cheap, structural case: where the modules are free,
  sheaf-\(\otimes\) coincides on \(U\) with presheaf-\(\otimes\) (sheafification is the
  identity on the already-sheafy free pieces), so the comparison is the elementary
  base-change isomorphism of free modules along the open immersion, requiring no
  general presheaf-pushforward adjunction. The PRIMARY proof obligation of this
  construction is therefore the free-cover specialisation of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, established \emph{without} the Mathlib-absent
  general adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)
  (``H1'', see \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). As a fallback, should that
  free-cover restriction-compatibility genuinely require H1, the by-hand associator
  has not in fact gotten smaller, and one would instead obtain associativity by
  comparing against the superseded route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to
  \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}).
\end{proof}

The four group axioms are discharged exactly as in Mathlib's
  \(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\)
  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}), where on a skeletal
  monoidal category each law is fed a \emph{single} isomorphism via the skeletality
  map \(\mathtt{hC} : (X \cong Y) \to X = Y\):
  \[
    \mathtt{one\_mul} := \mathtt{hC}\,\langle \lambda_X \rangle,
    \quad
    \mathtt{mul\_one} := \mathtt{hC}\,\langle \rho_X \rangle,
    \quad
    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} := \mathtt{hC}\,\langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
    \quad
    \mathtt{mul\_comm} := \mathtt{hC}\,\langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle.
  \]
  Here the role of \(\mathtt{hC}\) is played by passage to iso-classes: an
  isomorphism of sheaves becomes an equality of classes. Concretely, the unit laws
  are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is the associator
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each read as the proposition
  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\). The pentagon, triangle and hexagon
  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
  any of the four axiom proofs --- precisely as it is never invoked in the
  \(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) law fields above. This requires \emph{no}
  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and
  \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\): the
  group is assembled from the existence-of-isomorphism propositions alone, exactly
  as \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean})
  obtains its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) from
  \(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) whose monoid laws are
  the \(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) fields. The pinned carrier
  \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group structure;
  the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the pinned
  commit.
input.new_string:
The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
  \[
    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
    \quad
    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
    \quad
    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
    \quad
    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
  \]
  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
  any of the four axiom proofs.

  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
  pinned commit.

whiskering-stability lemmas below.

python3 -c "
s=open('blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex').read()
lines=s.split(chr(10))
hits=[i+1 for i,l in enumerate(lines) if 'monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal' in l]
out=[]
out.append('COUNT '+str(s.count('monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal')))
out.append('LINES '+str(hits))
for h in hits:

  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
  \[

    \quad
    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,

    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
    \quad
    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
  \]
  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
  any of the four axiom proofs.

  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law

  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):

  associativity is \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws are the unitors

  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. No pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is

  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse witness carried

  the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- with \emph{no}

input.content:
---
name: commring-pic-is-skeleton-route
description: Mathlib's CommRing.Pic IS the Skeleton-of-a-monoidal-category route, NOT a by-hand group — don't cite it as precedent for "group without a monoidal category"
metadata:
  type: project
---

In `Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean` (pinned commit, verified iter-216):
`CommRing.Pic R := Skeleton (Module.Invertible R)`, with
`instance : CommGroup (Pic R) := inferInstanceAs <| CommGroup <| Skeleton _`, and the
file DOES build `instance : MonoidalCategory (Module.Invertible R)`. So `CommRing.Pic`
is itself the `Skeleton` / `monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal` construction over a fixed ring —
it constructs a coherent monoidal category and takes its `Skeleton`.

The iter-216c directive asserted `CommRing.Pic` "builds a CommGroup directly on
Module.Invertible iso-classes WITHOUT constructing a monoidal category." That is FALSE
against the source. Do NOT repeat this phrasing in directives or blueprint prose.

The CORRECT framing (used in `Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex`): the project's
`tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid` must be built BY HAND precisely BECAUSE it CANNOT take
the `Skeleton` route — `Skeleton`/`monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal` require a coherent
`[MonoidalCategory]` (and `[BraidedCategory]`) on the carrier `X.Modules`, and that
coherent monoidal category for the VARYING structure sheaf is exactly what the
construction avoids. `CommRing.Pic` is the fixed-ring contrast (it CAN afford
`Skeleton`), not a same-pattern precedent.

**Why:** prevents re-introducing a source-contradicting citation that a strategy
critic already flagged once. **How to apply:** when citing Picard-group precedent,
cite `CommRing.Pic = Skeleton(Module.Invertible R)` as the fixed-ring `Skeleton`
route and frame the scheme-level construction as by-hand *because* no coherent
monoidal category on `X.Modules` exists. Relates to [[ts-assoc-flatness-gap]].
